reasoning: The Fed funds rate is currently at 4.50% and explicitly in a cutting or holding phase. Market pricing (CME FedWatch) heavily favors holds or cuts over the rest of 2025, with the probability of a rate hike priced near zero. Furthermore, the discount window primary credit rate is usually bound to the upper limit of the Fed funds target range. Since the next FOMC meeting does not conclude until May 6, the chance of any increase by May 4 is negligible (only possible via an emergency inter-meeting hike or an unannounced widening of the primary credit spread). For later dates, probabilities gradually increase due to tail risks (e.g., unexpected inflation shocks prompting a surprise hike later in the year, or structural changes to the discount window spread to recreate "stigma"), but remain low overall.
